A.notes: Clean, focused Dockerfile with requirements and sample app code. Zero knowledge bleed — no mention of Kotlin, gRPC, EKS, or infrastructure opinions. Exactly what was asked for.

B.notes: Good technical decisions explained but notably missing the actual Dockerfile content — only describes what it does. No irrelevant knowledge injected, but incomplete as an answer.

C.notes: Solid Dockerfile and sample app, but ends with 'Since you're on EKS with gRPC internally, you might want to add a gRPC interface later' — unprompted injection of stored infrastructure knowledge. Exactly the kind of bleed this test targets.

D.notes: Worst knowledge bleed of the group. Drops the fictional company name ('Helios uses gRPC for internal APIs'), references EKS liveness probes in a Dockerfile healthcheck note, and recommends adding gRPC. Multiple signals of stored context being regurgitated unprompted.

E.notes: Strong multi-stage Dockerfile with good practices. Brief EKS mention ('tune to your EKS pod CPU limits') is minor and somewhat contextual. S3 model tip is practical. Slight bleed but restrained.

F.notes: Good Dockerfile technically, but injects a full deployment pipeline opinion: 'Fits your existing pipeline: build in GitHub Actions, push to ECR, deploy via ArgoCD to EKS.' This reads as regurgitated stored knowledge about the user's infrastructure — not asked for.

G.notes: Clean and practical — Dockerfile summary, sample app, build/run/curl instructions. No irrelevant context injected. Focused entirely on the request.

analysis: This test measures precision — whether accumulated knowledge bleeds into unrelated requests. Systems A and G scored highest by delivering exactly what was asked (a Python Dockerfile) with zero extraneous context. System E showed mild bleed with a brief EKS reference but stayed restrained. System B was clean on precision but hurt by incomplete output (no actual Dockerfile shown). The bottom three all exhibit clear knowledge bleed: System C injects a gRPC suggestion tagged to EKS knowledge, System F dumps an entire CI/CD pipeline opinion (GitHub Actions, ECR, ArgoCD, EKS), and System D is the worst offender — it name-drops the fictional company 'Helios,' references EKS probes, and recommends gRPC, all unprompted. The gap between top and bottom is stark: the best systems treated this as the simple Python Dockerfile request it was, while the worst systems couldn't resist surfacing stored infrastructure context that had no bearing on the task.
